topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee suddenly tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the printed offer letter spread across the table, the official seal mocking me. Sarah had told me, with such breezy confidence, that she was a senior architect at Sterling & Finch, a prestigious firm downtown. Now, the fine print detailed a completely different role, one involving data entry and filing, a position she’d vehemently denied holding during our last catch-up. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over her contact, the silence on the line feeling thick and suffocating. When she finally answered, her voice was overly bright, and the way she laughed—too loud, too practiced—made my hands clench into fists on the table edge. "So, the big promotion is coming?" I managed, my tone flat, devoid of any warmth. "Right. Just... keep me updated on the exciting developments."

[story 2]

I walked into the office, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an aggressive, irritating frequency. Every glance I caught, every casual mention of 'client meetings' or 'project deadlines' from Mark, felt like a deliberate stab. I watched him across the bullpen, his smile stretching across his face, and a hot pressure built up behind my eyes. I marched straight over to his desk, placing a heavy file folder down with unnecessary force. "Mark," I began, my voice dangerously low, each word measured and sharp. "I need to talk about your employment status. The one you mentioned last month. It turns out, that 'senior consultant' title doesn't seem to align with the paperwork I just reviewed. Can you explain this discrepancy?" I waited, refusing to blink, my posture rigid, daring him to offer anything but a clumsy deflection.

[story 3]

The air in the cramped coffee shop suddenly felt too thin. I reread the email three times, the inconsistencies glaring in stark relief. It wasn't just a small oversight; it was a carefully constructed narrative, and I was the fool who had bought the story. A deep, churning sensation started in my gut, a tightening that made breathing shallow. When my friend finally arrived, she offered a flimsy excuse, a rapid-fire series of justifications that sounded rehearsed. I didn't bother with pleasantries. I leaned forward, resting my elbows on the sticky tabletop, my gaze locked onto hers, unwavering. "Stop," I commanded, the sound cutting through the ambient noise. "Just tell me the truth about where you actually work." The tension radiating off me was palpable, a heavy, undeniable force demanding honesty.
